Renewable energy, green energy or renewable energy is energy that humanity can have for an unlimited period of time and which, through its use, does not prejudice the environment and opportunities for future generations.
The most widely used forms of renewable energy are biomass, hydropower, wind energy, solar energy and geothermal energy.
Introduction Sometimes the terms renewable energy and renewable energy are used as synonyms.
A distinction is made by others: a renewable resource must be available for a virtually unlimited period of time, and a renewable energy source must also produce little environmental damage.
For example, there is a debate on the sustainability of the use of biomass as an energy source, but biomass is included among renewable energy sources.
In addition, other related terms are used, such as clean energy and green energy, with mostly the emphasis on environmental and climate impact.

The English term low carbon energy, 'low CO2 energy' also includes nuclear energy and the use of fossil fuels with CO2 capture and storage and is therefore not synonymous with renewable energy, but low CO2 energy is present in the context of energy sustainability.

For example, nuclear energy is included in the EU-Taxonomy for sustainable activities and the Dutch government is also committed to nuclear energy as part of the transition to a sustainable energy supply.
In order to combat global warming, lower CO2 emissions, including from electricity production, have become increasingly important.
Replacing fossil energy sources with renewable energy, i.e. the energy transition, is naturally given a lot of attention.
Based on a report from REN21 from 2017, renewable energy sources contributed 19.3% to global energy consumption and 24.5% to their generation of electricity in 2015 and 2016.

This energy is distributed as 8,9% from traditional biomass, 4,2% as heat energy (modern biomass, geothermal and solar heat), 3,9% from hydropower and the remaining 2,2% is electricity from wind energy, solar energy, geothermal and other forms of biomass.

Despite the increase in production and use of renewable energy, it was not possible to reduce the use of fossil fuels at global level in 2019; on the contrary, the use of fossil fuels is also increasing.
In a few places, this works a little better.
But even in Germany, which has a strong commitment to renewable energy, CO2 emissions are falling very little.

Definitions The United Nations defines renewable energy (renewable energy) as "energy from natural sources that are completed faster than consumed." Sustainable energy is considered by UN organisations in a broader context, from the definition of sustainability ('sustainability') from the Brundtland report: "to meet the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs." In the UN's global Sustainable Development Goals for Energy towards 2030, among others, universal access to affordable, reliable and modern energy services, reducing emissions, increasing the share of renewable energy and accelerating energy efficiency.
This takes account not only of environmental sustainability, but also of economic and social sustainability.
The World Bank RISE report identifies access to electricity, energy efficiency and renewable energy as the pillars of renewable energy.
For certain developing countries there is also access to cooking on clean energy.

The European Union defines renewable energy as "energy from renewable non-fossil sources, namely wind energy, solar energy (thermal solar energy and photovoltaic energy) and geothermal energy, environmental energy, tidal energy, wave energy and other energy from the oceans, hydropower, and energy from biomass, landfill gas, sewage treatment plant gas, and biogas." To define what the EU identifies as ecologically sustainable economic activities (including energy production), the EU-Taxonomy has been developed.

Leading in this, the "no serious harm" principle (Do No Significant Harm - DNSH) has been considered on the basis of six possible environmental impacts, including impacts on climate change and biodiversity.
On this basis, it is stipulated that for the time being, nuclear energy and natural gas in the EU are also considered sustainable investments.
The Dutch government follows the European Renewable Energy Directive (RED II) and therefore uses the same definition for renewable energy as the EU.
Renewable and renewable energy are regularly used as synonyms, among others by the National Agency for Entrepreneurship Netherlands and the Central Bureau for Statistics.
In the context of the sustainability of energy supply, renewable energy sources and other energy sources or measures are included.

For example, nuclear energy is part of the government's plans to achieve the climate targets and the Sustainable Energy Production and Climate Transition (SDE++) Stimulation provides subsidies not only for renewable energy sources but also for other activities that reduce CO2 emissions.
The International Energy Agency calculates energy from sun, wind, hydropower, biomass and specific smaller sources into renewable energy ('renewables').

The organisation places the concept of renewable energy (sustainable energy) in the broader context of sustainable development, as energy is closely linked to it in the areas of economy, environment and social welfare.
In the view of the Energy Agency, action in the field of renewable energy must strike a balance between these three dimensions.
This is in line with the interpretations of the United Nations and the European Union of Sustainable Development, based on the Brundtland report.
The Van Dale dictionary gives the word'renew' the meanings'renew' or 'become new again' (the word'renewable' is not included in the dictionary).
The meaning of'sustainable' is 'long-term', 'little wear and tear or spoilage' and/or 'low-impact on the environment'.

The Oxford English Dictionary dictionary for'renewables' ('renewable energy sources') gives the meaning "types of energy that can be replaced naturally, such as wind or water energy" and for'sustainable' ('sustainable') the meanings "using natural products and energy in a way that does not harm the environment" and "which can continue or continue for a long time."

History Since the first oil crisis in 1973, research has been carried out in the Netherlands on alternative technologies to make better use of natural and inexhaustible energy sources such as wind, sun, tides and waves, including the Energy Research Centre Netherlands (ECN) in Petten and the TNO Institute for Environment, Energy and Process Innovation.
In particular, this type of research was viewed with some suspicion in the circles of the oil companies, partly because of the fear of partially losing control of the energy market.

Some oil companies, such as Shell, have themselves stepped into this type of research, although with between 6 and 10% of the company's total investment, these investments are still very lagging behind fossil fuel investments.
Geopolitical impact Since 2010, there has been an increasing debate about the geopolitical impact of the increasing use of renewable energy.
It is expected that former fossil fuel exporters would weaken their geopolitical power, while countries with high sunshine, wind, hydropower or geothermal sources would strengthen it.
Countries rich in critical technologies for renewable energy are also expected to gain more geopolitical power.
The GeGaLo index of geopolitical gains and losses assesses how the geopolitical position of 156 countries can change if the world is completely switched to renewable energy sources.
Former exporters of fossil fuels are expected to lose in the index, while the positions of former importers of fossil fuels and countries rich in renewable energy sources are expected to improve.
Forms of renewable energy Solar panels convert sunlight into electricity via PV cells.
This electricity is also called solar power.
Thermal solar power.
This is a general term for three types of energy applications.
The previously mentioned solar boiler and space heating, solar radiation from windows in buildings, so use without special devices, and concentrated solar power (CSP).
The latter is particularly applicable in southern countries.
CSP is the concentration of sunlight by means of mirrors on a medium to be heated, which in turn is used to generate steam for the propulsion of turbine generators.
A technique that is exclusively, but very useful for large-scale power generation, such as the solar power plant at Kramers Junction, California (USA) which has been in operation since the mid-1980s.
Solar collectors convert sunlight into heat.

Especially inexpensive, large versions, such as pipes in asphalt, external walls and roofing, combined with heat storage in groundwater and a heat pump can lead to huge CO2 reductions in the Netherlands and Belgium.
Solar tower.
By solar heat, a constant air flow is developed in a vertical pipe.
This system can have an output equal to that of a conventional power plant.
Passive solar energy - passive methods for the extraction of solar energy.
These include houses and buildings that are built in such a way that the sun can directly heat up the spaces, for example with large windows on the sun side and small windows on the shade side.
However, it is wise to think of a way to keep the sun out in the summer.
For example, this can be a tree with foliage.
But also a crossing where the sun shines under in winter and not in summer.
This crossing can therefore be used to install solar panels for example.
Photoelectrochemical cells.
These have instead of two layers of solid as electrodes, a semiconducting solid as electrode and a liquid like the other.
They can use sunlight directly to decompose water into hydrogen and oxygen.
The English term PEC (photolectro chemistry) is used to refer to these techniques.
Of course, this only applies as a sustainable form of energy if the raw materials are not made from fossil fuels.
Wind Energy A wind turbine uses the power of the wind to power an electric generator to generate electricity or to initiate a movement, such as an old grain mill.
Wind is caused by air currents flowing from a high pressure area to a low pressure area.
The air pressure differences are caused by variations in the amount of solar radiation on the earth's surface.
Wind energy can be divided into wind energy on land and at sea.
On land wind energy is cheaper, but there is less room for windmills.
At sea, the winds are more constant and louder.
Wind energy is also used directly as a thrust for sailing ships and since 2016 there are plans and experiments to equip cargo ships with sailing.
Bioenergy Biomass is controversial as a renewable energy source.
When biomass is burned, CO2 is released, as with natural gas, oil and coal.
The idea with biomass is that the CO2 released is again absorbed by growing forest or other organic material.
Biomass does not run out as a result of the growth of the propellant, and is a renewable source, unlike natural gas, oil and coal.
The discussion is about whether this works in practice.
Organic products can be used directly as fuel, supplied with flammable gas by fermentation or converted into ethanol by refining and other chemical techniques.
Different generations can be distinguished.
The first generation mainly used sugar, starch and oil.
These parts of the plant could in many cases have been used for human consumption, and this generation is often referred to as one of the causes of rising food prices.
The second generation uses a wider range of biomass, such as plant residues and stool.
More advanced technology is needed for this than for the first generation.
As a third generation, energy is referred to as algae.
For algae less surface is needed than for plants, possibly they can also be harvested from surface water.
Geothermal energy Geothermal energy or geothermal heat.
Getting heat out of the face of the earth.
The interior areas of the Earth are warmer than the Earth's surface.
Continuously flows an estimated 42 million Megawatt of heat out.
This heat can be used directly to heat buildings, but also to generate electricity in a geothermal power plant.
Historically, the oldest geothermal power stations are built in places where the magma is relatively close to the earth's surface, but in principle other sites are also eligible.
Geothermal water reservoirs are found from a few dozen meters to several kilometres below the earth's surface.
Depending on their production capacity, small (300 kW to 10 MW), medium (10 to 50 MW) and large (50 to 100 MW and above) geothermal power stations are distinguished.
The design of a geothermal power plant depends on the nature of the geothermal source.
A dry steam plant uses an underground reservoir in which steam is already under high pressure.
A flash station uses an underground reservoir of overheated water which is converted into steam in the plant.
A binary power station uses a primary circuit of water of less than 100°C to drive a secondary circuit in which a liquid circulates at a lower boiling point.
Energy from the sea and rivers Gulf energy.
The movement of the waves of the sea is used to generate electricity.
Tidal energy.
The differences in water height of the tides are converted into electric energy via turbines.
Blue energy.
This is energy that can be generated by the difference in salt concentration between seawater and freshwater.
Hydro.
This is energy from height differences of water, usually due to the construction of a dam or a natural waterfall.
OTEC (abbreviated from the English ocean thermal energy conversion).
This technique uses the temperature difference between surface water and the deeper layers of the ocean to generate electricity.
Variable renewable energy solutions There is a plethora of solar and wind energy worldwide, but these are not always available locally.
The demand for electricity does not stop if there is no sunshine and the wind is quiet (this is called a dunkellaute).

In order to make a reliable energy system with weather-dependent sources, the following solutions are in place: International power connections Many European countries are working together on an integrated European energy market.
The various national electricity networks are increasingly interconnected.
TenneT, the Dutch network operator, is also working on expanding the connections with abroad.
New cables are being built to Germany (1.5 GW power) and Denmark (0.7 GW), and existing connections with Germany and Belgium are being expanded.
In 2024, TenneT expects to be able to import or export up to 8.4 GW with these connections.
By comparison, in that year TenneT expects the Netherlands to own 36 GW of operational power, of which just under half use wind/sun/water as an energy source (17 GW).

Nevertheless, meteorological data is also known where a Dunkelflaute occurred simultaneously across almost all of Europe, which makes it difficult to capture with international connections when most neighbouring countries are dependent on wind and solar energy.

Spare capacity Many countries have established capacity systems with the aim of keeping sufficient conventional power plants open to ensure security of supply, including Belgium, Germany, France and the United Kingdom.

These schemes mean that certain electricity producers are guaranteed revenue by the government for a number of years, so that it remains profitable to keep the power plants open and continue to invest in them.
In view of climate change, it is important that in the long term reserve capacity is made up of renewable energy sources as much as possible.
Biomass plants, for example, can generate sustainable energy without being dependent on weather conditions.
Energy storage With the storage of excess generated electricity, peaks and declines in electricity supply and demand can be collected.
Currently (2018) no infrastructure has been built that can store such quantities of electricity, but there are several projects and initiatives that explore the possibilities.
Pumping plant The most commonly used method for electricity storage in electricity networks in the world is the use of pumping plants.
In the case of a surplus of electricity, water is pumped to a higher basin.
When demand for electricity increases, the basin is emptied along a turbine that generates electricity.
This technique is particularly used in countries with a naturally high altitude difference.
In the Netherlands and Belgium there are plans for pumping plants which operate by means of a ring dike in the sea or an underground basin.
In the Netherlands, the Plan Lievense was designed to use the Markermeer for energy storage in this way.
Power-to-gas Gas is easier to store than electricity and can also be used immediately in many existing gas infrastructure.
A surplus of electricity can be used to extract hydrogen from water, through electrolysis.
This hydrogen can then be used in different ways.
This hydrogen can later be used to generate energy (the so-called hydrogen economy).
This can be done with a fuel cell or by burning hydrogen.
For example, hydrogen can also be added to natural gas, reducing the use of natural gas.
The addition of carbon dioxide can also produce synthetic methane.
The different processes in which electrical energy is converted into chemical energy are called power-to-gas.
Electric cars The expectation is that more and more people will have electric cars in the future.
The battery accumulators of these motor vehicles can be used to cope with shortages on the power grid.
In the event of a shortage during a Dunkelflaute, electric cars that are not used can return the electricity from their batteries to the power grid.
The battery of an average electric car could power a household for two weeks.
Smart grids By sending demand for electricity, the risk of shortages can be reduced and available electricity distributed more efficiently.
Moreover, energy is expected to become more and more decentralised in the future.
Smart grids enable better alignment between grid operators, producers and consumers.
For example, when information on peak taxes and high energy prices can be automatically passed on to connected devices, they can only be switched on under a certain electricity price.
Smart grids can thus also optimise the power consumption during a Dunkelflaute for the situation.
Several companies work together to create a universal standard for smart grids, the Universal Smart Energy Framework (USEF).
Sustainable energy in Europe In 2005, the European Commission launched the project for a European Energy Union, in which renewable energy plays an important role.
With Directive 2009/28/EC, the European Union has planned to meet at least 20% of its total energy needs by 2020 through renewable energy.
This objective is achieved through national targets, involving all Member States at least 10% of their transport fuels from renewable sources by 2020.
Sustainable energy in the Netherlands The Netherlands had committed itself at European level in 2016 to obtain 14% of its energy consumption from renewable sources in 2020.
This figure was 11% by the end of 2020.
When looking only at electricity production, the percentage of renewables was 26% by 2020.
In 2021, 33% of total electricity production came from renewable sources.
With this, the Netherlands is lagging behind its commitments under the Energy Agreement for Sustainable Growth.
In 2022, the percentage had increased to 40%.
In 2024, the renewable share grew by 4% to 54% of the generated electricity.
At the beginning of the economic crisis, the percentage of renewable energy increased as total energy consumption decreased and, above all, coal plants reduced their production.
According to TNO and Utrecht University, the Dutch government has devoted decades to the growth of the fossil sector, putting the Dutch economy in a vulnerable position.
Some steps were taken at the beginning of the 21st century for a renewable energy mix, such as the use of biofuels in traditional coal plants (0.1% of energy in 2018).
Other techniques, such as wind, water and solar energy, are increasingly being used (2.3% of energy in 2018).

For several years more wind energy has been generated in the Netherlands, because the production costs of windmills per kilowatt hour have been reduced by the large investments made by Germany and, to a lesser extent, by Denmark in technical developments.
The cost of solar energy also decreased during this period.
Government policy In order to stimulate renewable energy in the Netherlands, the government has established the Sustainable Energy Production Incentive Scheme (SDE+) since 2011.
It is financed by a storage facility called Storage Renewable Energy (ODE), which is levied on the consumption of electricity and natural gas (in addition to the energy tax).
The production of sustainably generated electricity or green electricity grew very slowly in the Netherlands compared to that in other European countries.

One reason for this is constantly changing legislation in the field of energy, for example the temporary abolition of subsidies after the Purple II cabinet and the dependence of the Dutch government on gas benefits and the traditional oil industry in the port of Rotterdam.
The Dutch government collects about 20% of its income from the fossil sector and sectors that depend on the fossil sector, and therefore has an interest in its survival.

Another factor is geological; the Netherlands does not have much sun, little height differences (water power), little tectonic activity (earth heat) and also the limited space to place windmills on land.

After decades of changing sustainable policies, the Netherlands had fallen into one of the most serious polluters in 2013 and, according to Germanwatch, the least sustainable country in the European Union.
In 2015, new coal plants were put into operation in the Netherlands, creating a national record of coal in the first half of this year.
Since many municipalities and provinces only take cheap gray electricity, several renewable energy companies have stopped tendering for tenders.
As a result of developments, the percentage of renewable energy produced decreased in 2015.
In that year, CO2 emissions increased by 5%.
Due to a lawsuit of the citizens' movement Urgenda, the Dutch government is committed to the target of at least 25% sustainable production by 2020.
The Rutte II government had just lowered its target to 14%: the absolute minimum required by the European Union.
However, this percentage would no longer be feasible.
In 2015 Minister Kamp started the national roll-out of smart meters in order to tax sustainable energy production.
These meters cannot be reduced, causing solar panels owners to lose up to 80% of their income due to the absence of a refund.
The province of Noord-Holland announced at the end of October 2019 that it is investing 4.5 million euros in solar energy projects.
This money is primarily used to enable more solar energy on roofs, parking facilities and industrial sites.
In addition, it looks at how solar panels can be used along provincial roads and cycle paths, at provincial bridges and tunnels and on the province's own buildings.
Experiment local energy projects Until 2014, legislation and regulations were based on large-scale and central energy generation.
From 2015, the legislation was relaxed for ten small (up to 500 connections) and ten large (up to ten thousand connections) projects per year.
In this way, the government aims to stimulate local initiatives for renewable energy generation.
Renewable energy in Belgium Belgium has committed itself at European level to achieving 13% of its energy from renewable sources by 2020.
In 2012, this percentage was 6.8%.
In 2017, it had grown to 9.1%.
The Belgian Government has decided to phase out nuclear energy until 2025.
The additional energy demand will be compensated by a mix of renewable and fossil energy sources.
Renewable energy costs Of all these types of renewable energy, biomass, hydropower and geothermal energy have traditionally been economically competitive, depending on location.
Wind energy from large wind turbines, usually in wind farms for the production of sustainable electricity.
Electricity from a wind farm at sea was still about 3 times more expensive, in 2009, but costs are falling rapidly.
In 2018 Chinook/Vattenfall won the first grantless tender.
Windpark Hollandse Kust provides clean energy for 1 million residential homes without the need for subsidies.
Heat for solar heating in buildings and homes (e.g. with large windows "to the south").
Anno 2021 is solar energy cheaper than conventional energy.

The trend for solar panels and windmills to become cheaper and fossil fuel to become more expensive due to carbon-pricing will increase this difference and in 2030 it will no longer be profitable to keep fossil fuel plants in production.
The German Fraunhofer Institute expects the solar panels combined with battery storage calculated in Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) to be competitive with fossil energy in 2022.
Emerging species are: Solar heat combined with very well insulated homes and buildings.
There are already central heating boilers on the market that prefer to use heat from solar collectors for space heating, and only switch to gas when solar heat is too short.
